React gives every JSX element a set of event props —

content.text: The Golden Rule: Pass, Don't Call
The single most common beginner mistake is accidentally calling the handler during render instead of passing it to React.

Three Ways to Write an Event Handler
React accepts any of these equivalent forms:
Form 3 — an inline arrow that calls the named function with arguments — is essential whenever you need to pass data (like an item ID) to the handler. The arrow function closes over
React's event system wraps the native browser event in a
— a cross-browser wrapper with the same interface as the DOM event. Your handler receives this object as its first argument.

React exposes camelCase versions of all standard DOM events. These are the most frequently used:
By convention, handler functions are named with a
prefix, and the prop they attach to uses an
prefix. This makes it easy to see at a glance which functions are event handlers and which props accept them.
When a component exposes an event callback as a prop (so the parent can react to something that happens inside the child), name that prop with the
DOM events bubble up the element tree. A click on a button also triggers
on every ancestor. React preserves this behavior.
Some elements have default behaviors that you often want to suppress. The most common is a form submission reloading the page. Call
at the start of the handler.

shows the browser menu (prevent to show a custom context menu).

Handling onChange for Different Input Types

This component combines click, change, submit, keyboard, and focus events to build a simple comment input with character counter.
Mistake 1: Using addEventListener instead of React props
inside JSX or a component body (outside an effect) creates a new subscription on every render and is never cleaned up. Always use React's event props for events on React-managed elements.
Mistake 2: Forgetting to read the right property for checkboxes
Mistake 3: Creating new handler functions needlessly inside JSX

Inline arrow functions in JSX create a new function object on every render. For most cases this is fine, but inside lists it means every item gets a new function reference — a potential source of unexpected re-renders in heavily optimized components. Extract to a named function when the handler has more than one line.
to simulate interactions and assert the resulting state or DOM changes.

React event props only work on HTML elements; custom components must forward them to an HTML element inside.
